common.legalDisclaimer: ImmiFina is not a bank, licensed financial advisor, or legal service. All content is for educational purposes only. Consult a licensed financial professional for advice specific to your situation.
common.benefitsDisclaimer: Benefit eligibility estimates are approximate and based on general program guidelines. Eligibility varies by state and individual circumstances. Always verify with official government sources.
common.remittanceDisclaimer: Fees and exchange rates are illustrative examples only, not live quotes. Compare providers before you send money.

onboarding.title: Set up your profile
onboarding.intro: A few steps so we can personalize guidance, forecasts, and your next steps.
onboarding.s1Lead: What best describes your current status in the U.S.?
onboarding.optCitizen: U.S. citizen — you have full citizenship
onboarding.optGreen: Permanent resident (Green Card) — right to live and work permanently
onboarding.optVisa: Visa holder — here on a temporary visa (work, student, etc.)
onboarding.optDaca: DACA recipient — Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals
onboarding.hasSsn: I have a Social Security Number (SSN)
onboarding.whatSsnBody: A Social Security number is a 9-digit ID used for work, taxes, and credit in the U.S. Not everyone has one right away.
onboarding.whatItinBody: An Individual Taxpayer Identification Number lets you file taxes without an SSN. It is not a work authorization by itself.
onboarding.yearsUs: How long have you lived in the U.S.?
onboarding.s2Lead: What is the most important financial thing you want to do right now?
onboarding.s4Lead: Monthly amounts help us personalize forecasts and tips. Estimates are OK.
onboarding.incomeHint: Money that actually hits your account after deductions. If paid weekly: hourly × hours × 4.3 per month (approx.).
onboarding.canSave: I can save this much per month

landing.heroTitle: Your financial future, in plain language
landing.heroSubtitle: Understand your money and what to do next — built for immigrants and first-generation Americans navigating the U.S. financial system for the first time.
landing.stats1: 1 in 5 U.S. households sends money abroad each year.
landing.stats2: Millions of newcomers build credit from zero — the right steps matter early.
landing.stats3: Many benefit programs exist; knowing where to look changes outcomes.
landing.problemTitle: Most finance apps were built for someone else
landing.problemBody: They assume years of U.S. credit history, fluent financial English, and products you already recognize. ImmiFina meets you where you are — with forward-looking tools and patient explanations.
landing.featForecastDesc: Project savings with conservative assumptions and see milestones ahead.
landing.featCreditDesc: Understand how scores work and explore a gentle simulator — no hard pull.
landing.featBenefitsDesc: See programs you may want to research further, with links to official sources.
landing.featRemitDesc: Compare representative fees and speeds across major providers.
landing.trustLead: We know immigrants and first-generation families are careful with personal information — so we’re transparent about how ImmiFina works.
landing.trustDataBody: We use secure sign-in and encryption in line with common industry practice. You choose what you enter; we use it only to power your forecasts, guidance, and assistant experience in the app.
landing.trustNoBankBody: You never have to link a bank account to use ImmiFina. There’s no Plaid or read-only bank login in v1 — only what you choose to type in.
landing.trustNoSellBody: We don’t sell your personal information to data brokers or advertisers. If we offer optional services later, they won’t depend on selling your profile.
landing.how1Body: Income, expenses, and goals stay private; we use them to personalize guidance.
landing.how2Body: Forecasts and guides translate complex systems into clear next steps.
landing.how3Body: Chat in English, Spanish, or Chinese (Simplified) for calm, educational answers.
landing.langBody: English, Español, and 中文 (简体) at launch — same product, your choice.
landing.ctaBannerBody: We're building carefully. Leave your email for product news — no spam.

auth.name: Full name
auth.forgotBody: Password reset by email is not enabled in this preview. If you need access, register a new account or contact support.
auth.invalidCredentialsHint: If login works on your computer but not here, Vercel must use the same Supabase URL and service role key as your local .env.local — or create a new account on this site.
auth.authDbError: Could not read the user database. Check Supabase keys in Vercel (no extra spaces) and redeploy.
auth.authSecretMissing: Add AUTH_SECRET in Vercel → Environment Variables (a long random string, 32+ characters), save for Production, then redeploy. Without it, sign-in cannot set a session cookie.
auth.registerDbError: Could not create your account in the database. Open Supabase → SQL and run the script from supabase/schema.sql, then try again.
auth.serviceUnavailable: The app can't reach the database. In production, add NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY in Vercel → Settings → Environment Variables, redeploy, and ensure your Supabase SQL schema is applied.
auth.genericError: We couldn't complete that request. Try again.
auth.rateLimited: Too many attempts. Please wait and try again.
auth.emailTaken: An account with this email already exists.
auth.passwordRules: At least 8 characters with uppercase, lowercase, and a number.
auth.agreeToTerms: By creating an account, you agree to our <terms>Terms of Service</terms> and <privacy>Privacy Policy</privacy>.

dashboard.journeyLead: Here's where you are on your financial journey.
dashboard.foundationHint: Educational score from your saved profile — not a credit or bank rating.
dashboard.foundationHowIntro: We add up to 100 points from items in your profile. Update anything in Settings.
dashboard.foundationPt2: 25 points — monthly income is set and monthly expenses are entered
dashboard.foundationPt3: 15 points — current savings amount is entered
dashboard.foundationPt4: 15 points — a main financial goal is selected
dashboard.foundationPt5: 10 points — state of residence is set
dashboard.foundationPt6: 5 points — you noted an SSN or ITIN in Settings
dashboard.foundationPt7: Up to 5 points — emergency savings vs one month of expenses (we scale savings ÷ expenses, up to 5 points when savings cover a full month)
dashboard.nextStepsLead: Based on your situation, here is what to explore first.
dashboard.surplusWarning: Your expenses are higher than income in this snapshot. Review categories in Forecast or ask the assistant for budgeting ideas.
dashboard.quickForecast: In 5 years, your savings could be about
dashboard.quickForecastNote: (4% annual growth, surplus saved monthly — educational estimate).
dashboard.emergencyBody: First milestone: save one month of expenses ({amount}).
dashboard.noChats: No conversations yet — start one from AI Assistant.
dashboard.situationHint: If your immigration status or main financial goal changes, update them in Settings — your next steps and guidance will adjust.

forecast.title: Financial forecast
forecast.subtitle: Understand your money, then see a simple projection — educational only, not a guarantee.
forecast.understandIncome: Your monthly income here is what you actually receive after taxes — what lands in your account.
forecast.payFrequencyHint: Paid weekly? Rough monthly = hourly × hours × 4.3. Biweekly × 2.17. Semi-monthly × 2.
forecast.expenseBreakdownLead: Splitting expenses shows where money goes. Totals update automatically.
forecast.saveRuleBody: A common guideline is 50/30/20 — needs, wants, then savings/debt. Even small consistent amounts help.
forecast.twentyPctLine: 20% of your income would be about {amount}/month — use the slider for what feels realistic.
forecast.sliderSaveLabel: I can save this much per month
forecast.forecastExplainBody: We use a conservative 4% annual return and your chosen monthly savings. The gray line shows growth if you add $0 per month (only your starting balance compounds).
forecast.chartSaving: If you save this amount monthly
forecast.chartTarget: If you hit your monthly target
forecast.milestoneEnd: After {years} years at this pace, about {amount} saved.
forecast.milestoneEmergency: Emergency fund (3 months expenses): about {months} months to reach at your surplus pace.
forecast.milestoneDown: Illustrative 20% down on a $400k home ($80k): about {years} years at your surplus pace.
forecast.msEmergency: Emergency fund (3× monthly expenses ≈ {amount}): ~{months} months to reach at this save rate.
forecast.ms5k: $5,000 saved milestone: ~{months} months at this pace.
forecast.msDown: Illustrative 10% down payment target ({amount}): ~{years} years at this pace (median home varies by state).
forecast.aiHint: We send your inputs to our assistant for educational ideas — not investment advice.

credit.title: Credit guide
credit.phase_no_historyBody: No U.S. score yet is normal for many newcomers. You are not “bad” — you are not on file yet.
credit.phase_buildingBody: You have started establishing accounts. On-time payments matter most now.
credit.phase_establishingBody: You have a track record. Keep utilization low and avoid missed payments.
credit.phase_optimizingBody: You are in a stronger position — focus on rates, mix, and long-term habits.
credit.whatBody: A credit score is a number lenders use to estimate how likely you are to repay debt. It is built from your credit report — not from income or savings.
credit.whatBodyExtended: Scores usually range from 300–850. Think of it as a reputation signal for borrowing — not a judgment of your worth.
credit.rangePoor: 300–579: Poor — harder to rent or borrow affordably.
credit.rangeFair: 580–669: Fair — some options, often higher rates.
credit.rangeGood: 670–739: Good — many lenders will work with you.
credit.rangeVg: 740–799: Very Good — better rates and approvals.
credit.invisible: When you first arrive you may be “credit invisible” — no U.S. file yet. That is common and fixable with the right first products.
credit.whyImmigrantBody: Without a U.S. score, renting, phone plans, and loans can be harder — even if you were financially responsible abroad. U.S. history does not transfer.
credit.guidedLead: Answer a few plain questions — we map to a rough educational range, not your real score.
credit.gqAccounts: Do you have any U.S. credit accounts right now?
credit.gqOldest: How long have you had your oldest U.S. credit account?
credit.gqMissed: Have you ever missed a payment in the U.S.?
credit.gqPayFull: Do you pay credit card balances in full each month?
credit.gqPayNa: I don't have a card yet
credit.gqInq: Hard credit applications in the last 6 months?
credit.guidedDisclaimer: Your real score from a bureau may differ. This is not a credit pull.
credit.payment: Payment history — paying on time matters most.
credit.utilization: Credit utilization — how much of your limit you use.
credit.age: Length of credit history — older accounts help when managed well.
credit.mix: Credit mix — a healthy blend of cards and installment loans.
credit.inquiries: New credit — many hard inquiries in a short window can lower scores.
credit.assessBody: This is a self-check, not a score pull. Honest answers help you pick the right first steps.
credit.planCitizen: Citizen / long-term resident: consider a no-annual-fee card, autopay, and keeping utilization low.
credit.planVisa: Visa holder: confirm issuer policies; secured cards and credit-builder products are common starting points.
credit.planOther: Other: focus on records you can document and products designed for thin or no credit file.
credit.stepSecured: Secured credit card — refundable deposit, lower risk for the issuer.
credit.stepBuilder: Credit-builder loan — small installment reported to bureaus.
credit.stepAU: Authorized user — if a trusted person adds you responsibly to their account.
credit.simHint: Adjust sliders to see a rough educational range — not your real score.
credit.qNew: Are you new to U.S. credit?
credit.qSecured: Have you opened a secured card or credit-builder product?

benefits.title: Benefits finder
benefits.subtitle: Explore programs that match your situation. Always confirm with official sources.
benefits.privacyLead: Your answers stay private and are used only to suggest programs. We do not verify immigration status — this is educational.
benefits.pregnantHint: Some programs focus on pregnant people and new parents.

eligibility.high: Strong match — worth reviewing official rules
eligibility.medium: Possible match — confirm with your state
eligibility.low: Less likely — still check if circumstances apply

medicaid.name: Medicaid / CHIP
medicaid.covers: Health coverage for low-income adults, children, pregnant women, elderly, and people with disabilities.
medicaid.elig: Often tied to Federal Poverty Level (FPL) and state rules.
medicaid.lang: Many states offer interpreter services; check your state Medicaid site.

snap.name: SNAP (food assistance)
snap.covers: Monthly benefits for groceries through an EBT card.
snap.elig: Income and asset tests vary by state; work rules may apply.
snap.lang: USDA and state sites offer multiple languages.

wic.name: WIC
wic.covers: Nutrition support for pregnant women, new parents, and young children.
wic.elig: Income at or below 185% FPL and nutritional risk criteria.
wic.lang: State WIC agencies provide multilingual materials.

liheap.name: LIHEAP
liheap.covers: Help with home energy bills and weatherization in some cases.
liheap.elig: Income guidelines set by state; funding is limited.
liheap.lang: Contact your state LIHEAP office for application help.

section8.name: Section 8 / Housing Choice Vouchers
section8.elig: Income limits vary by area and household size.
section8.lang: HUD and local Public Housing Agencies provide guidance.

headstart.name: Head Start / Early Head Start
headstart.covers: Early learning, health, and family support for young children.
headstart.elig: Generally for families at or below 100% FPL or receiving certain benefits.
headstart.lang: Programs emphasize family engagement in home languages.

schoolmeals.name: Free and reduced-price school meals
schoolmeals.covers: Breakfast and lunch at school for eligible students.
schoolmeals.elig: Household income or participation in certain programs.
schoolmeals.lang: Your school district handles applications — ask for translated forms.

remittance.title: Remittance comparison
remittance.subtitle: Illustrative comparison across major providers — verify before you send.
remittance.introBody: Sending money home is a regular decision for many families. Small differences in fees and exchange rates add up over a year — compare what your family actually receives, not just the “fee.”
remittance.bankHelp: Bank transfer: best when your family has a bank account; often cheaper for larger amounts; may take a few days.
remittance.cashHelp: Cash pickup: fast and widely available; your family picks up cash with ID; fees can be higher.
remittance.walletHelp: Mobile wallet: instant in some countries; requires the right app on your family’s phone.
remittance.tip1: Compare before every transfer — rates change daily.
remittance.tip2: Watch “$0 fee” offers — the exchange rate may be worse.
remittance.tip3: Larger amounts sometimes get better rates.
remittance.tip4: Weekend transfers can have worse rates.
remittance.tip5: Keep records for taxes and your own notes.
remittance.tip6: Recurring transfers may earn loyalty discounts with some providers.

chat.title: AI Assistant
chat.empty: Start a conversation. Replies are educational, not personal financial advice.
chat.error: Could not get a reply. Try again.
chat.rateLimited: You're sending messages too quickly. Please wait a moment.
chat.historyEmpty: No saved chats yet. Send a message to start one.

settings.title: Settings
settings.name: Full name
settings.situationLead: Update when your situation changes — for example if you naturalize, get a green card, or shift your top financial priority.
